title: The world of street art and care-filled design
speakers: Ella Rochelle-Lawton (Project & Program Manager, First Amendment)
key_points: Design is never isolated, it exists in public space, in relationship to people, and in conversation with the world. The closing call to action: ask 'whose voice isn't in the room yet?' and invite them in to shape what comes next.

Designing for public space changes how you make: a different relationship to permanence (you can't undo it, can't predict how it's experienced) shifts focus from 'platforms, pixels, or print' to 'people, place, and presence.'

Street art models behaviors design lacks: it's conversational (artists respond to, build on, and challenge each other's work) and involves checking back in, artists return to clean, restore, and revamp (e.g. Os Gemeos returning to SF years later to honor passed artists), versus design's hand-off-and-done model.
Design can include or exclude, she contrasts community murals signaling 'this space belongs to us' (Tenderloin) with hostile architecture like uncomfortable benches meant to deter unhoused people.

Cited the Porchlight Mural Program (evaluated by Yale School of Medicine): collaborative mural-making neighborhoods showed increased social cohesion, sense of agency, better neighborhood conditions, and feelings of safety, plus decreased mental health stigma, proving public art impact is more than aesthetic.

quotes.text: As designers, we have to acknowledge that our work can include or exclude, create belonging or remove it.
quotes.talk: The world of street art and care-filled design

quotes.text: Street art isn't just a medium. It's a way of working, a way of responding, and a way of connecting people.
quotes.talk: The world of street art and care-filled design
